api/Utils.Mathematics.Expressions.html.title: Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Expressions | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Mathematics.Expressions.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Expressions Classes ExpressionComparer Provides a custom equality comparer for Expression objects. The comparison involves simplifying each expression via an ExpressionSimplifier before checking for structural and semantic equivalence. ExpressionDerivation Provides single-variable symbolic differentiation for LINQ expression trees. ExpressionExtensions Provides extension methods for simplifying LINQ expression trees, as well as custom exceptions for extension-related errors. ExpressionExtensionsException Represents validation errors that occur while building derivative expressions. ExpressionIntegration Provides integration rules for mathematical expression trees. ExpressionSimplifier Provides methods to simplify or rewrite expression trees by applying various transformations, typically related to arithmetic identities or constant folding. This partial class works alongside other parts of ExpressionSimplifier to compose a complete transformation pipeline. MathExpressionExtensions Provides helper methods to derive lambda expressions using ExpressionDerivation.

For instance, Round(1234, 2) rounds to the nearest multiple of 100. public static T Round<T>(T value, int exponent = 0) where T : struct, INumber<T>, IPowerFunctions<T> Parameters value T Value to be rounded. exponent int The exponent of 10 used as the rounding base. Defaults to 0 (which rounds to integer). Returns T A value rounded to the nearest power-of-ten multiple. Type Parameters T A numeric type that supports exponentiation via IPowerFunctions<TSelf>. Round<T>(T, T) Rounds value to the nearest multiple of base. If value is exactly between two multiples, it is rounded up. public static T Round<T>(T value, T @base) where T : struct, INumber<T> Parameters value T Value to be rounded. base T Rounding base. Must be non-zero. Returns T The value of value rounded to the nearest multiple of base. Type Parameters T A numeric type supporting modulus and comparison operators. Exceptions DivideByZeroException Thrown if base equals zero.

api/Utils.Mathematics.NullableIntEx.html.summary: Class NullableIntEx Namespace Utils.Mathematics Assembly Utils.dll Provides helper methods for working with nullable numeric endpoints that can represent open ranges by treating null as positive or negative infinity as needed. public static class NullableIntEx Inheritance object NullableIntEx Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Methods Compare<T>(T?, T?, bool) A specialized compare for intersection checks (start <= end). If isMinForComparison => treat null as -∞ for 'start' else treat null as +∞ for 'end'. This is an attempt to unify a compare approach, but keep in mind logic can get subtle with infinite endpoints. public static int Compare<T>(T? left, T? right, bool isMinForComparison) where T : struct, IBinaryInteger<T>, IComparable<T> Parameters left T? right T? isMinForComparison bool Returns int Type Parameters T Decrement<T>(T?) Decrements an endpoint if finite. If already null => -∞ or +∞? We have to interpret usage. This method is used for subtracting an interval: we want (r2.Min - 1) etc. But if r2.Min = null => that's -∞ => (r2.Min - 1) => -∞ => no difference. public static T? Decrement<T>(this T? x) where T : struct, IBinaryInteger<T>, IMinMaxValue<T> Parameters x T? Returns T? Type Parameters T GreaterOrEqual<T>(T?, T?) Compares a >= b. Null => +∞ is always >= any finite. public static bool GreaterOrEqual<T>(T? a, T? b) where T : struct, IBinaryInteger<T>, IComparable<T> Parameters a T? b T? Returns bool Type Parameters T Increment<T>(T?) Increments an endpoint if finite. If x is +∞ => stays +∞, if x is -∞ => stays -∞. public static T? Increment<T>(this T? x) where T : struct, IBinaryInteger<T>, IMinMaxValue<T> Parameters x T? Returns T? Type Parameters T LessOrEqual<T>(T?, T?) Compares a <= b. Null is interpreted as +∞ here for convenience. If a is +∞ => then a <= b is false unless b is also +∞. public static bool LessOrEqual<T>(this T? a, T? b) where T : struct, IBinaryInteger<T>, IComparable<T> Parameters a T? b T? Returns bool Type Parameters T Less<T>(T?, T?) Compares a < b. api/Utils.Mathematics.html.title: Namespace Utils.Mathematics | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Mathematics.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Mathematics Classes DigitListType Represents a collection of digit definitions for a particular group of numbers. DigitType Describes how a specific digit should be rendered and composed within a number name. FloatingPointComparer<T> Provides floating-point comparisons that allow for small deviations using a configurable interval. FractionListType Represents a collection of fractional definitions. FractionType Configures how fractional values should be rendered. GroupType Represents a numbered group of digits used when decomposing large numbers. GroupsListType Collection wrapper for GroupType entries. LanguageType Describes the conversion settings for a single language or culture. MathEx Provides extended mathematical functions and utilities, including custom rounding, clamping, and Pascal's triangle generation. NullableIntEx Provides helper methods for working with nullable numeric endpoints that can represent open ranges by treating null as positive or negative infinity as needed. NumberListType Collection wrapper for NumberType records to simplify XML serialization. NumberScale Represents the scale used for large number names (e.g., thousand, million). NumberScaleType Configures the prefixes and metadata needed to build number scale names. NumberToStringConverter Provides functionality to convert numbers to their string representation according to a specific culture or custom configuration. NumberToStringConverter.ReplacementRule Represents a configured replacement rule. NumberType Describes a fixed mapping between a numeric value and its literal representation. Numbers Represents the root configuration element that aggregates number conversion definitions for multiple languages. ReplacementType Defines a string replacement applied to the generated number name. ReplacementsListType Holds a list of string replacements applied after number conversion. StaticNamesType Holds the static names mapped to particular scales. SuffixesType Describes the suffixes appended to scale names. Trigonometry<T> Provides a base implementation of IAngleCalculator<T> for custom angle measures such as Degrees or Grades, along with default trigonometric/hyperbolic implementations. Interfaces IAngleCalculator<T> Provides an interface for angle-based calculations in various units (Radians, Degrees, Grades). Includes standard and extended trigonometric, hyperbolic, and inverse functions. INumberToStringConverter Converts numeric values into a formatted string representation while exposing the largest supported number for validation purposes. Enums ReplacementScope Defines how a replacement should be applied to the converted value.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The DNSCanonicalWriter scans assemblies (via DNSFactory) for DNSResponseDetail types marked with DNSRecordAttribute. It then generates expression-based writers that serialize each DNS element's fields into the DNS wire format. When writing a DNS packet: The DNS header is written first, updating record counts (QDCount, ANCount, etc.). Each request record is written, translating its string Type to a numeric code (requestClassTypes). Each response record is written, including the user-defined RData portion. The final output is a byte[] representing the DNS packet to be sent over the network or stored. Constructors DNSCanonicalWriter(IEnumerable<DNSFactory>) Initializes a new instance of the DNSCanonicalWriter class, scanning the given DNSFactory collections for DNS record detail types. public DNSCanonicalWriter(IEnumerable<DNSFactory> factories) Parameters factories IEnumerable<DNSFactory> A collection of DNSFactory instances to load DNS types from. Returns byte[] The serialized output. Write(DNSResponseRecord) Converts the provided DNS response record into its canonical on-the-wire representation. public byte[] Write(DNSResponseRecord record) Parameters record DNSResponseRecord The DNS response record to serialize. Returns byte[] The trimmed byte buffer that contains the canonical DNS payload.

api/Utils.Net.DNS.DNSConstants.html.title: Class DNSConstants | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Net.DNS.DNSConstants.html.summary: Class DNSConstants Namespace Utils.Net.DNS Assembly Utils.Net.dll Constants representing DNS datagram flags and fields masks. public static class DNSConstants Inheritance object DNSConstants Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Remarks The bit layout within the DNS header defined in RFC 1035 §4.1.1 is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ID | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ |QR| Opcode |AA|TC|RD|RA| Z|AD|CD| RCODE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | QDCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ANCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | NSCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ARCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Each mask below targets the corresponding field within this layout. Fields AuthenticDatas indicates in a response that all the data included in the answer and authority portion of the response has been authenticated by the server according to the policies of that server. public const ushort AuthenticDatas = 32 Field Value ushort AuthoritativeAnswer Authoritative Answer bit. Set to 1 if the responding server is an authority for the queried domain. public const ushort AuthoritativeAnswer = 1024 Field Value ushort CheckingDisabled indicates in a query that Pending(non-authenticated) data is acceptable to the resolver sending the query. public const ushort CheckingDisabled = 16 Field Value ushort Error Error field. Indicates the type of DNS error encountered in the response. public const ushort Error = 15 Field Value ushort See Also DNSError MessageTruncated Message Truncated bit. Set to 1 if the DNS message was truncated during transmission. public const ushort MessageTruncated = 512 Field Value ushort OpCode OpCode (Operation Code) field. Indicates the type of DNS query. public const ushort OpCode = 30720 Field Value ushort See Also DNSOpCode QR QR (Query/Response) bit. Set to 1 for Response, 0 for Query. public const ushort QR = 32768 Field Value ushort See Also DNSQRBit RecursionDesired Recursion Desired bit. Set to 1 if the client requests a recursive DNS query. public const ushort RecursionDesired = 256 Field Value ushort RecursionPossible Recursion Possible bit. Set to 1 if the server supports recursive queries. public const ushort RecursionPossible = 8 Field Value ushort ReservedZ Reserved Zero (Z) bits. Reserved for future use. Should be set to 0. public const ushort ReservedZ = 64 Field Value ushort

This constructor is suitable for constructing outgoing DNS packets. public DNSDatagram() DNSDatagram(byte[]) Initializes a new instance of the DNSDatagram class using the provided byte array. public DNSDatagram(byte[] data) Parameters data byte[] The byte array containing DNS data. Remarks Sets the internal buffer to data and the Length to the array's length. Useful for parsing existing DNS data. DNSDatagram(Stream) Initializes a new instance of the DNSDatagram class by reading data from the provided stream. public DNSDatagram(Stream dataStream) Parameters dataStream Stream The stream containing DNS data. Remarks Reads up to 512 bytes from the dataStream into the internal buffer. The Length property is set to the number of bytes actually read. Properties Length Gets the current length of the datagram, representing the total number of bytes written. public int Length { get; } Property Value int Position Gets the current reading position within the underlying byte buffer. public int Position { get; } Property Value int Remarks This is updated automatically as bytes or data structures are read from the buffer. Methods ReadByte() Reads a single byte from the datagram at the current Position. public byte ReadByte() Returns byte The byte that was read. ReadBytes(int) Reads a specified number of bytes from the datagram at the current Position. public byte[] ReadBytes(int length) Parameters length int The number of bytes to read. Returns byte[] A byte array containing the bytes that were read. ReadString() Reads a DNS-formatted string (domain name) from the datagram at the current Position, using the default encoding. public string ReadString() Returns string The domain name that was read, or null if the string is empty. ReadString(Encoding) Reads a DNS-formatted string (domain name) from the datagram at the current Position, using the specified encoding. public string ReadString(Encoding encoding) Parameters encoding Encoding The encoding used to parse label bytes into characters. Returns string The domain name that was read, or null if the string is empty. ReadUInt() Reads a 32-bit unsigned integer from the datagram at the current Position. public uint ReadUInt() Returns uint The 32-bit unsigned integer that was read. ReadUShort() Reads a 16-bit unsigned integer from the datagram at the current Position. public ushort ReadUShort() Returns ushort The 16-bit unsigned integer that was read. ResetRead() Resets the Position property to zero, allowing the datagram to be read again from the beginning. public void ResetRead() ToBytes() Converts the content of this datagram to a new byte array containing all written bytes. public byte[] ToBytes() Returns byte[] A byte array representing the datagram's written data. Write(byte) Writes a single byte into the datagram's buffer, and advances Length by one. public void Write(byte b) Parameters b byte The byte to write. Write(byte[]) Writes the entire contents of a byte array into the datagram's buffer, and advances Length. public void Write(byte[] bytes) Parameters bytes byte[] The byte array to write. Write(int, ushort) Writes a 16-bit unsigned integer into the datagram at a specified position, without changing the Length. public void Write(int position, ushort value) Parameters position int The position where the value should be written. value ushort The 16-bit value to write. Write(string) Writes a string in DNS label format using ASCII encoding by default, supporting DNS name compression. public void Write(string s) Parameters s string The string to write (domain name in dot notation). Write(string, Encoding) Writes a string in DNS label format using the specified Encoding, supporting DNS name compression. public void Write(string s, Encoding encoding) Parameters s string The string to write (domain name in dot notation). encoding Encoding The text encoding used to convert string labels to bytes. Write(ushort) Writes a 16-bit unsigned integer into the datagram at the current Length position. public void Write(ushort value) Parameters value ushort The 16-bit value to write. Write(uint) Writes a 32-bit unsigned integer into the datagram at the current Length position. public void Write(uint value) Parameters value uint The 32-bit value to write.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks This comparer dynamically generates comparison and hashing functions for each unique derived DNSElement type. It leverages expression trees to analyze fields and properties, ensuring that two DNS elements of the same derived type are compared field by field (including arrays), or via an IEquatable<T> interface implementation if available. When generating hash codes, each relevant field or array element is considered. This mechanism allows for a high degree of flexibility and extensibility when new DNS types or fields are introduced. Properties Default Gets the default, shared instance of the DNSElementsComparer. public static DNSElementsComparer Default { get; } Property Value DNSElementsComparer Remarks The default instance is suitable for most scenarios where a single global comparer is required for DNSElement objects. Methods Equals(DNSElement, DNSElement) Determines whether the specified objects are equal. public bool Equals(DNSElement x, DNSElement y) Parameters x DNSElement The first object of type T to compare. y DNSElement The second object of type T to compare. Returns bool true if the specified objects are equal; otherwise, false. GetHashCode(DNSElement) Returns a hash code for the specified object. public int GetHashCode(DNSElement obj) Parameters obj DNSElement The object for which a hash code is to be returned. Returns int A hash code for the specified object. Exceptions ArgumentNullException The type of obj is a reference type and obj is null.

interpret the query. NameError = 3 Name Error - Meaningful only for responses from an authoritative name server, this code signifies that the domain name referenced in the query does not exist. NotImplemented = 4 Not Implemented - The name server does not support the requested kind of query. Ok = 0 No error condition Refused = 5 Refused - The name server refuses to perform the specified operation for policy reasons. For example, a name server may not wish to provide the information to the particular requester, or a name server may not wish to perform a particular operation (e.g., zone transfer) for particular data. ServerFailure = 2 Server failure - The name server was unable to process this query due to a problem with the name server.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A default singleton instance is available via Default. Additional instances can be built from specified assemblies or types. Instances may be combined with the + operator to aggregate multiple DNSFactory configurations into one. Constructors DNSFactory() Initializes a new instance of the DNSFactory class by scanning the assembly that contains this type for DNS-related classes. public DNSFactory() Remarks Discovers all types in the same assembly that derive from DNSResponseDetail and bear at least one DNSRecordAttribute. DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<Assembly>) Initializes a new instance of the DNSFactory class by scanning the given assemblies for DNS-related classes. public DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<Assembly> assemblies) Parameters assemblies IEnumerable<Assembly> A collection of Assembly instances to scan. Remarks This constructor only sets the DNSTypes property. It does not populate internal mappings unless you combine it with another factory. For a fully initialized factory, see DNSFactory() or use operator +(DNSFactory, DNSFactory) to merge with another factory. DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<Type>) Initializes a new instance of the DNSFactory class by scanning a set of types for DNS-related classes (those derived from DNSResponseDetail and tagged with DNSRecordAttribute). public DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<Type> types) Parameters types IEnumerable<Type> A collection of Type objects to scan. Remarks This constructor only sets the DNSTypes property. It does not populate internal mappings unless you combine it with another factory. For a fully initialized factory, see DNSFactory() or DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<Assembly>). DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<DNSFactory>) Initializes a new instance of the DNSFactory class by aggregating one or more DNSFactory instances into a single combined factory. public DNSFactory(params IEnumerable<DNSFactory> factories) Parameters factories IEnumerable<DNSFactory> A collection of DNSFactory instances to combine. Remarks The aggregated factory merges the mappings from all specified factories. If duplicates occur for the same (DNSClassId, ID) or (DNSClassId, name) pair, the last entry encountered overwrites previous ones. Properties DNSTypes Gets a read-only list of all discovered DNS response types (those implementing DNSResponseDetail and annotated with DNSRecordAttribute). public IReadOnlyList<Type> DNSTypes { get; } Property Value IReadOnlyList<Type> Remarks Only set if you call a constructor that explicitly scans assemblies or a provided collection of types. Default Gets the default DNSFactory instance, scanning the current assembly for DNS types annotated with DNSRecordAttribute. public static DNSFactory Default { get; } Property Value DNSFactory Methods GetClassId(DNSClassId, string) Retrieves the recorded ID for a specific (DNSClassId, record name) combination. public ushort GetClassId(DNSClassId @class, string className) Parameters class DNSClassId The DNSClassId. className string The record name. Returns ushort The stored record ID. GetClassName(DNSClassId, ushort) Retrieves the recorded name for a specific (DNSClassId, ushort) combination. public string GetClassName(DNSClassId @class, ushort classId) Parameters class DNSClassId The DNSClassId. classId ushort The record identifier. Returns string The stored record name. GetDNSType(DNSClassId, string) Retrieves the DNS response Type for a specific (DNSClassId, record name) combination. public Type GetDNSType(DNSClassId @class, string className) Parameters class DNSClassId The DNSClassId. className string The record name. Returns Type A Type that implements DNSResponseDetail. GetDNSType(DNSClassId, ushort) Retrieves the DNS response Type for a specific (DNSClassId, ushort) combination. public Type GetDNSType(DNSClassId @class, ushort classId) Parameters class DNSClassId The DNSClassId. classId ushort The record identifier. Returns Type A Type that implements DNSResponseDetail. Operators operator +(DNSFactory, DNSFactory) Combines two DNSFactory instances into a new factory that merges their mappings. public static DNSFactory operator +(DNSFactory left, DNSFactory right) Parameters left DNSFactory The first DNSFactory. right DNSFactory The second DNSFactory. Returns DNSFactory A new DNSFactory containing the merged mappings of left and right.

If Length is an integer, it represents a fixed size. If it is a string, the named property or field is used to determine this size dynamically. The Condition property allows specifying an expression or condition that determines whether this field should be read or written. Utils.Net.DNS.DNSHeader.Flags: A collection of DNS flags and codes packed into a 16-bit field (e.g., QR, OpCode, AA, etc.). Counts of queries, answers, authority records, and additional records (see Utils.Net.DNS.DNSHeader.QDCount, Utils.Net.DNS.DNSHeader.ANCount, Utils.Net.DNS.DNSHeader.NSCount, Utils.Net.DNS.DNSHeader.ARCount). The wire format defined in RFC 1035 §4.1.1 (extended by RFC 2535) is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ID | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ |QR| Opcode |AA|TC|RD|RA| Z|AD|CD| RCODE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | QDCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ANCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | NSCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ARCOUNT | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ RFC 2535 introduces the AD (Authenticated Data) and CD (Checking Disabled) bits. Clients should only trust AD when the upstream server is trusted, and the CD flag allows requesting unsigned data for local validation. Constructors DNSHeader() Initializes a new instance of the DNSHeader class with a randomly generated ID and default QR bit set to Question. public DNSHeader() Properties Additionals Gets a list of DNS additional records associated with this header. public IList<DNSResponseRecord> Additionals { get; } Property Value IList<DNSResponseRecord> AuthenticDatas Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Authentic Data (AD) bit is set. public bool AuthenticDatas { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks This bit (introduced in RFC 2535) indicates that all data included in the answer and authority sections of the response has been authenticated by the server according to the server's policies. AuthoritativeAnswer Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Authoritative Answer (AA) bit is set. public bool AuthoritativeAnswer { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks This bit is set in responses when the responding server is an authority for the domain in question. Authorities Gets a list of DNS authority records associated with this header. public IList<DNSResponseRecord> Authorities { get; } Property Value IList<DNSResponseRecord> CheckingDisabled Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Checking Disabled (CD) bit is set. public bool CheckingDisabled { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks This bit (introduced in RFC 2535) is set in a query to indicate that Pending (non-authenticated) data is acceptable to the resolver sending the query. ErrorCode Gets or sets the error code (RCODE) in the DNS header. public DNSError ErrorCode { get; set; } Property Value DNSError Remarks This indicates the status of the response (e.g., FormatError, ServerFailure, etc.). ID Gets or sets the 16-bit DNS packet identifier. [DNSField] public ushort ID { get; set; } Property Value ushort MessageTruncated Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Message Truncated (TC) bit is set. public bool MessageTruncated { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks If true, the total size of the DNS packet exceeded the allowable size, and the message was truncated. OpCode Gets or sets the DNSOpCode portion of the DNS flags, indicating the requested operation (e.g., Standard query, Inverse, Status). public DNSOpCode OpCode { get; set; } Property Value DNSOpCode QrBit Gets or sets the DNSQRBit (query or response) portion of the DNS flags. public DNSQRBit QrBit { get; set; } Property Value DNSQRBit RecursionDesired Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Recursion Desired (RD) bit is set. public bool RecursionDesired { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks In a query, setting this bit requests that the DNS server perform recursive resolution on behalf of the client. RecursionPossible Gets or sets a value indicating whether the Recursion Available (RA) bit is set. public bool RecursionPossible { get; set; } Property Value bool Remarks In a response, if this bit is set, the DNS server supports recursive queries. Requests Gets a list of DNS question records associated with this header. public IList<DNSRequestRecord> Requests { get; } Property Value IList<DNSRequestRecord> ReservedFlags Gets or sets the portion of the header reserved for future use (Z bits). public byte ReservedFlags { get; set; } Property Value byte Remarks Historically, these bits must be zero, but with RFC 2535, some of them were repurposed for AD/CD. Responses Gets a list of DNS answer records associated with this header. public IList<DNSResponseRecord> Responses { get; } Property Value IList<DNSResponseRecord> Methods Append(DNSHeader) Merges (appends) the requests, responses, authority, and additional records from another DNSHeader into the current one, provided the IDs do not match. public void Append(DNSHeader header) Parameters header DNSHeader Another DNSHeader to merge from. Exceptions Exception Thrown if the header has the same ID as the current header. ToString() Returns a string describing the current header fields and associated request/response sets. public override string ToString() Returns string A human-readable string representation of this DNS header.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The DNSPacketWriter scans assemblies (via DNSFactory) for DNSResponseDetail types marked with DNSRecordAttribute. It then generates expression-based writers that serialize each DNS element's fields into the DNS wire format. When writing a DNS packet: The DNS header is written first, updating record counts (QDCount, ANCount, etc.). Each request record is written, translating its string Type to a numeric code (requestClassTypes). Each response record is written, including the user-defined RData portion. The final output is a byte[] representing the DNS packet to be sent over the network or stored. Constructors DNSPacketWriter(IEnumerable<DNSFactory>) Initializes a new instance of the DNSPacketWriter class, scanning the given DNSFactory collections for DNS record detail types. public DNSPacketWriter(IEnumerable<DNSFactory> factories) Parameters factories IEnumerable<DNSFactory> A collection of DNSFactory instances to load DNS types from. Properties Default Gets the default DNSPacketWriter instance, which scans the DNS types from Default. public static DNSPacketWriter Default { get; } Property Value DNSPacketWriter Methods Write(DNSHeader) Serializes the provided DNS header into the target transport representation. public byte[] Write(DNSHeader header) Parameters header DNSHeader The DNS header to serialize. Returns byte[] The serialized output.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks This class stores fields such as: Name: The domain name being queried. Utils.Net.DNS.DNSRequestRecord.RequestType: A 16-bit identifier for the query type (e.g., 0x01 for A records). Class: The DNS class, typically IN or ALL. It also provides an explicit Type property representing the record type in string format. The Clone() method returns a shallow copy of this record’s essential fields. The query section wire layout from RFC 1035 §4.1.2 is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / NAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | TYPE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | CLASS | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Constructors DNSRequestRecord() Initializes a new instance of the DNSRequestRecord class. public DNSRequestRecord() DNSRequestRecord(string, string, DNSClassId) Initializes a new instance of the DNSRequestRecord class with the specified type, name, and optionally, a DNS class. public DNSRequestRecord(string type, string name, DNSClassId @class = DNSClassId.ALL) Parameters type string The string identifier for the requested record type (e.g., "A", "AAAA"). name string The domain name being queried. class DNSClassId The DNS class for the query (default is ALL). Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when type or name is null. Properties Class Gets or sets the DNS class of the request (e.g., IN or ALL). [DNSField] public DNSClassId Class { get; set; } Property Value DNSClassId Name Gets or sets the domain name being queried. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName Name { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Type Gets or sets the string identifier for the requested DNS record type (e.g., "A", "AAAA", "MX"). public string Type { get; set; } Property Value string Remarks This property is not directly part of the DNS wire format; rather, it determines Utils.Net.DNS.DNSRequestRecord.RequestType internally when the record is serialized. Methods Clone() Creates a new object that is a copy of the current instance. public object Clone() Returns object A new object that is a copy of this instance. ToString() Returns a string that represents the current object. public override string ToString() Returns string A string that represents the current object.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A DNS response record includes: The queried Name (DNSDomainName). The numeric Class of the response (e.g., 1 for A, 28 for AAAA), stored in Utils.Net.DNS.DNSResponseDetail.ClassId. The DNS class (ClassId), typically IN. The TTL, or time-to-live, indicating how long the record may be cached. An RDLength (2 bytes) indicating the size of the resource data. The RData, which is a DNSResponseDetail object representing the record-specific data (e.g., IP addresses, mail server, etc.). The wire layout in RFC 1035 §4.1.3 is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / NAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | TYPE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | CLASS | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | TTL | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | RDLENGTH | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--| / RDATA / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Constructors DNSResponseRecord() Initializes a new instance of the DNSResponseRecord class. public DNSResponseRecord() DNSResponseRecord(string, uint, DNSResponseDetail) Initializes a new instance of the DNSResponseRecord class with the specified domain name, TTL, and record details. public DNSResponseRecord(string name, uint TTL, DNSResponseDetail details) Parameters name string The domain name to which the record pertains. TTL uint The time-to-live for this record. details DNSResponseDetail A concrete DNSResponseDetail subclass representing the record data. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown if name or details is null. Properties Class Gets or sets the numeric record type for this DNS response. Typically set by RData's Utils.Net.DNS.DNSResponseDetail.ClassId. [DNSField] public ushort Class { get; } Property Value ushort ClassId Gets or sets the DNS class for this response (commonly IN). [DNSField] public DNSClassId ClassId { get; } Property Value DNSClassId Name Gets or sets the DNS domain name associated with this response record. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName Name { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName RDLength Gets or sets the length of the RData section, in bytes. This is determined during serialization. [DNSField] public ushort RDLength { get; } Property Value ushort RData Gets or sets the DNSResponseDetail describing the record data (RDATA). Setting this value updates Class to reflect the RData's Utils.Net.DNS.DNSResponseDetail.ClassId. public DNSResponseDetail RData { get; set; } Property Value DNSResponseDetail TTL Gets or sets the time-to-live (TTL) for this record, indicating how long it may be cached. [DNSField] public uint TTL { get; set; } Property Value uint Methods Clone() Creates a new object that is a copy of the current instance. public object Clone() Returns object A new object that is a copy of this instance. Remarks The returned object is an anonymous type containing copies of the basic fields. The RData is also cloned, calling Clone() to duplicate its content fields. ToString() Returns a multi-line string describing this response record, including its domain name, class, TTL, and the specific RData fields. public override string ToString() Returns string A human-readable summary of this DNS response record.

determined by the AddressFamily of the stored IPAddress object. If the address family is unsupported, a NotSupportedException is thrown. Internally, this class overrides Utils.Net.DNS.DNSResponseDetail.ClassId and Name to ensure the wire-format ID matches the address type. Properties IPAddress Gets or sets the IPAddress associated with this record. If the address family is not IPv4, IPv6, or Osi, a NotSupportedException is thrown. public IPAddress IPAddress { get; set; } Property Value IPAddress Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown when an address family outside of InterNetwork, InterNetworkV6, or Osi is assigned. Name Gets the human-readable DNS record name from the DNSRecordAttribute or defaults to the class name if none is specified. public override string Name { get; } Property Value string Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown if the IP address family is not InterNetwork, InterNetworkV6, or Osi. Methods ToString() Returns the string representation of the underlying IPAddress. public override string ToString() Returns string The IP address in dotted-decimal or colon-hex notation.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A CNAME record indicates that the specified CName is the canonical or primary name for the alias represented by the record’s owner name. For example, if "www.example.com" is a CNAME to "example.com", then "www.example.com" is the alias, and "example.com" is the canonical name. CNAME records do not directly trigger additional section processing in standard DNS resolution. However, a DNS server may internally restart the resolution at the canonical name, as described in RFC 1034 §3.6.2. This class corresponds to a DNS record with type code 0x05 and DNS class IN. The RDATA layout is simply the canonical domain name: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / CNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ The CNAME value is a <domain-name> specifying the canonical target. Properties CName Gets or sets the canonical domain name to which the owner name is aliased. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName CName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Methods ToString() Returns the canonical (target) name of this CNAME record. public override string ToString() Returns string The string representation of CName, or an empty string if CName is null.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks In standard DNS, record type 0 is undefined/reserved. This class may serve as a fallback for unrecognized record types or experimental usage. If you intend to store actual TXT records, consider using the official TXT record type code (0x10, decimal 16) instead. The illustrative RDATA layout is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / DATAS / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ The payload is interpreted as raw bytes. TXT records normally use type code 16 (0x10), but this fallback keeps the unparsed data accessible for experimentation. Properties Datas Gets or sets the raw data payload associated with this fallback record. [DNSField] public byte[] Datas { get; set; } Property Value byte[] Methods ToString() Returns a textual representation of the bytes stored in Datas. public override string ToString() Returns string A string that lists each byte as a hexadecimal value enclosed in brackets. The caller must ensure Datas is not null before calling this method.

Traditionally, this is stored as two separate character strings (CPU and OS). However, this example consolidates both into a single Info field. If you prefer to store CPU and OS separately, you can replace Info with two string fields annotated with DNSFieldAttribute. Example (traditional two-field approach): [DNSField] public string CPU { get; set; } [DNSField] public string OS { get; set; } The wire-format RDATA (RFC 1035 §3.3.2) is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / CPU / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / OS / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Each field is a <character-string>. CPU describes the processor type, and OS identifies the operating system. See RFC 1010 for common values. Properties Info Gets or sets a string containing CPU and OS information. This example uses a single field, but you may split it into two fields if desired (CPU and OS). [DNSField] public string Info { get; set; } Property Value string Methods ToString() Returns the entire stored text in Info. public override string ToString() Returns string

Resolvers would perform additional section processing to locate an A record for that host. However, the MD record type is marked as obsolete because mail routing has long been replaced by MX records, as detailed in RFC 974. The recommended policy upon encountering an MD record is to treat it as an MX record with a preference of 0. This class is annotated with [Obsolete] and [DNSRecord(DNSClass.IN, 0x03)] for completeness but should not be used in modern DNS scenarios. The obsolete RDATA format is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / MADNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ MADNAME is the mail agent host; resolvers historically fetched its address. This class is annotated with [DNSRecord(DNSClass.IN, 0x04)] for the IN class and type code 4, but also marked [Obsolete] to indicate it should not be used in modern DNS configurations. The RDATA layout (now obsolete) is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / MADNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ MADNAME is a <domain-name> pointing to a host that accepts mail for the owner and forwards it. Older resolvers performed additional section processing to fetch address records for that host. Properties MadName Gets or sets the DNSDomainName that indicates the host (MADNAME) responsible for mail forwarding for the domain. Since DNSDomainName is a struct, it cannot be null, but it can hold a default (empty) value. The RDATA format consists of two domain names: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / RMAILBX / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / EMAILBX / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Both fields are <domain-name> values identifying responsible and error mailboxes. Properties EMailBx Gets or sets the DNSDomainName of the mailbox that will receive error messages (EMAILBX). [DNSField] public DNSDomainName EMailBx { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName RMailBx Gets or sets the DNSDomainName of the mailbox responsible for the mailing list or mailbox (RMAILBX). Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks An MR record (type code 0x09) specifies a new mailbox domain name (NewName) for redirecting mail from an older mailbox name. It was once used for forwarding or renaming a mailbox, but modern DNS practice suggests using MX records for mail routing. This class is annotated with ObsoleteAttribute and [DNSRecord(DNSClass.IN, 0x09)], indicating that it should not be used in production scenarios due to obsolescence. The experimental RDATA layout is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / NEWNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ NEWNAME is a <domain-name> representing the replacement mailbox host. Properties NewName Gets or sets the new mailbox domain name. This is the name to which mail was originally meant to be redirected. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName NewName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Methods ToString() Returns the NewName string representation. Since DNSDomainName is a struct, it can't be null but can be empty if uninitialized. public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks Each MX record consists of: Preference: A 16-bit integer indicating priority. Lower values are preferred, meaning a mail transfer agent should try servers in ascending order of Preference. Exchange: A domain name specifying the mail exchange host (e.g., mail.example.com). For instance, if example.com has two MX records, one with preference 10 and another with preference 20, the sending mail server will first attempt the host with preference 10. If that fails or is unavailable, it tries the host with preference 20. The DNS library may perform additional processing (e.g., retrieving the A or AAAA address of the Exchange) when resolving MX records. The RDATA layout defined in RFC 1035 §3.3.9 is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | PREFERENCE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / EXCHANGE / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ PREFERENCE is the priority indicator (lower values preferred) and EXCHANGE is a <domain-name> identifying the target mail server. Implementations often follow up with address lookups for the exchange host. Properties Exchange Gets or sets the domain name of the mail exchange server (e.g. mail.example.com). [DNSField] public DNSDomainName Exchange { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Preference Gets or sets the 16-bit preference value for this MX record. A lower value indicates a higher priority in mail routing. [DNSField] public ushort Preference { get; set; } Property Value ushort Methods ToString() Returns a string representation containing the mail exchange name and its preference, for example: "mail.example.com (10)". public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The NS record stores the domain name (DNSName) of a host that should be authoritative for the owner name of this record. For example, if this record is under example.com, DNSName might be ns1.example.net, meaning ns1.example.net is an authoritative name server for example.com. Usage typically involves additional section processing within DNS responses to provide "glue records" (A or AAAA RRs) so resolvers can quickly find the IP addresses of these name servers. The wire-format RDATA layout defined in RFC 1035 §3.3.11 is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / NSDNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ The NSDNAME value is a <domain-name> that identifies a host which should be authoritative for the owner name. Additional section processing is usually performed to provide glue A or AAAA records for that host. Properties DNSName Gets or sets the domain name of the authoritative name server (e.g., ns1.example.com). [DNSField] public DNSDomainName DNSName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A NULL record is an experimental record type (code 0x0A) that carries opaque binary data in its RDATA field (Datas). This data may be up to 65535 bytes in length. Since the NULL RR is not intended for standard DNS usage, most resolvers will ignore or discard it unless specifically designed to handle custom data in DNS. Use of this record for production services is discouraged. The on-wire format simply stores arbitrary bytes: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / <anything> / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ The payload has no defined semantics; consumers must agree on meaning out-of-band. Properties Datas Gets or sets the raw binary data for this NULL record. May be up to 65535 bytes in length. [DNSField] public byte[] Datas { get; set; } Property Value byte[] Methods ToString() Returns a string consisting of the bytes in hexadecimal form, for example: "{ 54 78 1E 3A ... }". public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A PTR record is used to map (or "point") a domain name to another. Typically, PTR records are most commonly known for usage in the IN-ADDR.ARPA zone to perform reverse lookups, mapping IP addresses to hostnames. However, a PTR can also appear in other namespaces, simply indicating a pointer to another location in the domain space. Unlike CNAME records, PTRs do not imply special alias or canonical name behavior and cause no additional DNS processing for resolution. They are simply a data pointer. When placed in the IN-ADDR.ARPA domain, the name is usually the reverse-notation IP address (e.g., 1.2.168.192.in-addr.arpa), and the PTRName is the corresponding hostname (e.g., host.example.com). The RDATA format is the target domain name: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / PTRDNAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ PTRDNAME is a <domain-name> indicating the pointer destination. Properties PTRName Gets or sets the domain name to which this PTR record points. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName PTRName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Remarks For example, in a reverse DNS entry, PTRName would hold the canonical hostname associated with the IP's reverse-lookup domain. Methods ToString() Returns the string representation of PTRName. Because DNSDomainName is a struct, it will never be null, but may be an empty or default value if unset. public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The SOA record (type 0x06) is mandatory at the apex of every DNS zone. It includes: MName: The primary name server for this zone (the origin of zone data). RName: The mailbox of the person responsible for this zone, typically in the form of a domain name, e.g. hostmaster.example.com with a dot replacing the @. Serial: A 32-bit version number for the zone. Secondary name servers use this to detect zone updates. Refresh: The time interval in seconds before secondary name servers should check for updates from the primary. Retry: If a refresh attempt fails, the time (in seconds) to wait before retrying. Expire: The upper limit (in seconds) on how long a secondary may continue serving data if it cannot contact the primary. Minimum: The minimum TTL (in seconds) to be exported with any RR from this zone. Historically also used as the negative-caching TTL in some contexts. In typical zone files, the SOA record looks like: example.com. IN SOA ns1.example.com. hostmaster.example.com. ( 2023031501 ; Serial 10800 ; Refresh (3 hours) 3600 ; Retry (1 hour) 604800 ; Expire (7 days) 3600 ; Minimum (1 hour) ) The RDATA wire format is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / MNAME / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / RNAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | SERIAL | | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | REFRESH | | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | RETRY | | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | EXPIRE | | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | MINIMUM | | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ See RFC 1035 §3.3.13 for authoritative details. Properties Expire Gets or sets the expire value, in seconds, indicating how long a secondary may continue using the zone data if it cannot reach the primary. [DNSField] public uint Expire { get; set; } Property Value uint MName Gets or sets the domain name of the primary master name server for this zone. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName MName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Minimum Gets or sets the minimum TTL, in seconds, used as a lower bound for all resource record TTLs in the zone. [DNSField] public uint Minimum { get; set; } Property Value uint RName Gets or sets the mailbox domain name of the person responsible for this zone. Commonly written as something like "hostmaster.example.com" with a '.' in place of '@'. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName RName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Refresh Gets or sets the refresh interval, in seconds, that a secondary should wait before checking for updates from the primary. [DNSField] public uint Refresh { get; set; } Property Value uint Retry Gets or sets the retry interval, in seconds, for a secondary to wait if the refresh attempt fails. [DNSField] public uint Retry { get; set; } Property Value uint Serial Gets or sets the serial number of this zone. Incrementing this value indicates to secondaries that new data is available. [DNSField] public uint Serial { get; set; } Property Value uint Methods ToString() Returns a multi-line string describing the main parameters of the SOA record. public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A TXT record can contain one or more character-string segments, which can be combined into a single textual representation. In practice, many DNS libraries and servers treat TXT data as either a concatenated string or a list of separate segments. For example: example.com. 3600 IN TXT "Some descriptive text here" The Text property would hold the value "Some descriptive text here". Although RFC 1035 §3.3.14 states "one or more <character-string>s," many modern implementations treat this as a single text block. To conform to multi-segment usage, your library might need additional logic for splitting or concatenating strings if required by a specific environment. The wire format is a sequence of character strings: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / TXT-DATA / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Each TXT-DATA chunk begins with a length octet followed by that many bytes of text. Properties Text Gets or sets the text content of this TXT record. In many DNS scenarios, this is either a single string or a concatenation of multiple character-string segments. [DNSField] public string Text { get; set; } Property Value string Methods ToString() Returns the string content of the TXT record. public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The WKS record includes: A 32-bit IPv4 address (IPAddress) An 8-bit protocol number (Protocol). Examples: 6 (TCP), 17 (UDP). A variable-length bit map (Bitmap), with one bit per port. For instance, if Protocol=6 (TCP), the 26th bit corresponds to TCP port 25 (SMTP). If that bit is set, the service on port 25 is advertised as available. For each bit in Bitmap, the index corresponds to a port number. If the bit is 1, that port is available for the specified Protocol; otherwise 0 means unavailable. This approach is considered obsolete and rarely used in modern DNS contexts. Instead, SRV records or other service discovery methods are typically employed. Example usage (pseudocode): var wksRecord = new WKS { IPAddress = IPAddress.Parse("192.0.2.1"), Protocol = 6, // TCP Bitmap = new byte[] { 0x00, 0x01, ... } // Indicating ports 8, 9, etc. might be active }; WKS RRs do not trigger additional section processing. They are primarily for describing availability of TCP/UDP services on an IPv4 address, although they are considered effectively deprecated in modern DNS practice. The on-the-wire layout (RFC 1035 §3.4.2) is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | ADDRESS | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | PROTOCOL | | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | | | / <BIT MAP> / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ ADDRESS is the IPv4 address, PROTOCOL is the IP protocol number, and the bit map advertises active ports (nth byte covers ports n*8 to n*8+7). Properties Bitmap Gets or sets the variable-length bit map indicating which ports are available for this Protocol. The nth byte covers ports [n8..n8+7]. [DNSField] public byte[] Bitmap { get; set; } Property Value byte[] IPAddress Gets or sets the IPAddress for this WKS record. Must be IPv4 (AddressFamily.InterNetwork), as WKS does not support IPv6. public IPAddress IPAddress { get; set; } Property Value IPAddress Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown if an attempt is made to set an IPv6 address. Protocol Gets or sets the 8-bit IP protocol number (e.g., 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP). [DNSField] public byte Protocol { get; set; } Property Value byte Methods ToString() Returns a string showing the IPv4 address, the protocol number, and the bit map in hex bytes. public override string ToString() Returns string A textual representation for debugging/logging.

api/Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1035.html.title: Namespace Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1035 | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1035.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1035 Classes Address Represents an IP address record (A, AAAA, or NSAP) in DNS, as described by RFC 1035 §3.4.1 (A records), RFC 1886 (superseded but historically defining AAAA records), and the OSI NSAP address type. This class stores the IP address and reflects the correct numeric record ID based on the address family. CNAME Represents a DNS CNAME (Canonical Name) record, used to alias one domain name to another, as defined in RFC 1035 §3.3.1. Default Represents a fallback or "default" DNS record for scenarios where the record type is unrecognized, or when a custom processing path is desired for type code zero (which is reserved/unused). Despite the TXT-like comment, this class is annotated with [DNSRecord(DNSClass.IN, 0x00)], indicating it is treated as a placeholder for type 0. The raw payload is stored in the Datas property. HINFO Represents an HINFO (Host Information) record, as defined in RFC 1035 §3.3.2. MB Represents an MB (Mailbox) DNS record, as described in RFC 1035 §3.3.3 (marked as experimental). MD Represents an MD (Mail Destination) record, which is an obsolete DNS record type as per RFC 1035 §3.3.4. MF Represents an MF (Mail Forwarder) record, an older mail-routing DNS record type now considered obsolete per RFC 1035 §3.3.5. Similar to MD, the MF record has been superseded by MX. MG Represents an MG (Mail Group) record, classified as experimental in RFC 1035 §3.3.6. MINFO Represents a MINFO record, considered experimental and largely obsolete as per RFC 1035 §3.3.7. The MINFO record provides mail-related information (responsible mailbox, error mailbox) for a mailing list or mailbox owner. MR Represents an MR (Mail Rename) record, marked as experimental and obsolete as per RFC 1035 §3.3.8. MX Represents an MX (Mail Exchange) record in the DNS, as described in RFC 1035 §3.3.9 and further clarified by RFC 974. The MX record specifies a mail server responsible for accepting email on behalf of a domain, along with a preference value. NS Represents an NS (Name Server) record as specified in RFC 1035 §3.3.11. An NS record indicates the authoritative name server for a given domain. NULL Represents a NULL (experimental) DNS record as described in RFC 1035 §3.3.10. This record type can hold arbitrary data (up to 65535 bytes) and is primarily a placeholder for experimental or interim purposes. PTR Represents a PTR (Pointer) record in DNS, as specified by RFC 1035 §3.3.12. SOA Represents an SOA (Start of Authority) record, which defines zone-wide parameters including the primary name server, mailbox of the zone administrator, and various time intervals affecting caching and zone transfers, as described in RFC 1035 §3.3.13. TXT Represents a TXT record in DNS, as defined in RFC 1035 §3.3.14. TXT records store arbitrary human-readable text, often used for descriptive or application-specific data (e.g., SPF, DKIM, etc. in modern usage). WKS Represents a WKS (Well Known Services) record, as specified by RFC 1035 §3.4.2. A WKS record details which well-known services are available for a given IPv4 address and IP protocol (e.g., TCP or UDP).

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The AFSDB record indicates: Preference: A 16-bit integer specifying the priority (lower values are more preferred). AFSServer: The domain name of a host that provides AFS services for the owner name. A typical usage might look like: example.com. 3600 IN AFSDB 1 afsdbserver.example.com. This means that the domain example.com has an AFSDB record pointing to afsdbserver.example.com with priority 1 (preferred over higher numbers). The AFSDB record can also be used for other similar database services, depending on the subtype indicated by the Preference field. See RFC 1183 and related documents for more details on AFS usage and potential multi-server configurations. The wire format contains the preference followed by the server name: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | PREFERENCE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / SERVER / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Properties AFSServer Gets or sets the domain name of the AFS (Andrew File System) server. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName AFSServer { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Preference Gets or sets the 16-bit preference value for this AFSDB record. A lower number indicates a higher priority among multiple AFSDB records at the same owner name. [DNSField] public ushort Preference { get; set; } Property Value ushort Methods ToString() Returns a string showing the server name followed by the preference in parentheses, e.g. "afsserver.example.com (10)". public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks According to RFC 1183 §3.2, an ISDN record can contain: The ISDN phone number, in a digit string. An optional subaddress. However, this class currently only stores a single PhoneNumber field, and does not parse or serialize a subaddress if present. Consequently, it may be incomplete for scenarios requiring a subaddress or more detailed data. In modern DNS usage, ISDN records are virtually never used, and the specification in RFC 1183 is largely historical. Consult the RFC if you need a more comprehensive representation (including subaddresses). The record may store two character-strings: the ISDN number and an optional subaddress. +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / <ISDN-number> / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / <subaddress> (optional) / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Properties PhoneNumber Gets or sets the ISDN phone number. This record class does not handle any subaddress portion or other additional data fields. [DNSField] public string PhoneNumber { get; set; } Property Value string Remarks Since the original RFC 1183 format allows two distinct strings (ISDN number and optional subaddress), usage requiring a subaddress is not fully covered. Methods ToString() Returns the phone number as a string. Note that any subaddress is not supported in this minimal implementation. public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The RP record contains two domain names: MBoxDName: A domain name that specifies the mailbox for the responsible person. By convention, the first label of the name is used to replace the '.' with '@' to form a typical email address. For example, john.doe.example.com might map to john.doe@example.com. TxtDName: A domain name for a TXT record containing more information (e.g., about the responsible person). If this name points to the root (e.g., .), it indicates that no additional information is available. An example usage might be: example.com. 86400 IN RP john.doe.example.com. info.example.com. This indicates that john.doe@example.com is responsible for example.com, and info.example.com might hold a TXT record with further contact or host data. The wire format contains the two domain names consecutively: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / MBOXDNAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / TXTSDNAME / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Properties MBoxDName Gets or sets the domain name identifying the mailbox of the responsible person. By convention, the first label can be translated into an '@' character to form an email address, e.g. john.doe.example.com ? john.doe@example.com. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName MBoxDName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName TxtDName Gets or sets the domain name that points to a TXT record holding more information about the responsible person or host. If this is set to the root label ('.'), it indicates there is no additional information. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName TxtDName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Methods ToString() Returns a simple string containing the mailbox domain name and TXT domain name separated by a tab. For example: "john.doe.example.com info.example.com". public override string ToString() Returns string

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The RT record includes: Preference: A 16-bit integer specifying the relative preference of this route. Lower values indicate more preferred routes. DnsName: The domain name of the intermediate host or router that should be used for routing to the owner name of this record. Example usage might look like: example.com. IN RT 10 gateway.example.net. indicating that traffic to example.com should be routed through gateway.example.net with preference 10. Lower Preference values mean higher priority. The RT record does not trigger additional section processing (no extra A/AAAA records included by default). In modern networking, explicit routing via DNS is rarely practiced, and the RT record is largely historical. The wire layout is: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ | PREFERENCE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / EXCHANGER / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Properties DnsName Gets or sets the domain name of the intermediate host for routing traffic to the owner of this RT record. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName DnsName { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Preference Gets or sets the 16-bit preference value for this RT record. Lower numbers indicate a more preferred route. [DNSField] public ushort Preference { get; set; } Property Value ushort Methods ToString() Returns a string showing the preference and domain name, separated by a tab or space. For example: "10 gateway.example.net". public override string ToString() Returns string

This implementation only holds a single string for the PSDN, and does not attempt to parse any optional subaddress or additional fields. The wire format stores one character-string: +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / PSDN / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ Properties PSDN Gets or sets the PSDN (Public Switched Data Network) address as a string. api/Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1183.html.title: Namespace Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1183 | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1183.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Net.DNS.RFC1183 Classes AFSDB Represents an AFSDB (Andrew File System Database) record, as defined in RFC 1183 §1.2. This record helps locate AFS or related database servers for a particular domain. ISDN Represents an ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network) DNS record, as originally defined in RFC 1183 §3.2. This record stores an ISDN telephone number (and optionally a subaddress). RP Represents an RP (Responsible Person) record, as defined in RFC 1183 §2.2. This record identifies the mailbox of the person responsible for a particular DNS zone and an optional TXT record with additional information. RT Represents an RT (Route Through) record, as defined in RFC 1183 §2.3. The RT record specifies an intermediate host that should be used when routing to the owner of the record, along with a preference value. X25 Represents an X.25 PSDN address record, as defined in RFC 1183 §3.1 (type code 19). This record stores a single X.25 PSDN address (e.g., a numeric string like "311061700956").

This record is part of the DNS extensions that were designed to support OSI networking protocols and legacy network architectures. This record contains a single domain name field (DomainName), which represents the target domain associated with the NSAP address. Note that NSAP-PTR records are rarely used in modern DNS implementations. For additional details, see https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1348. Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The GPOS record is used to provide location information for a domain in a human-readable format. Each of the three fields (Longitude, Latitude, Altitude) is encoded as a string that represents a double-precision floating-point value. The string encoding is expected to follow the rules specified in RFC 1712. In this implementation, each field is annotated with a DNSFieldAttribute using the PrefixedSize1B option, which means that the length of the string is prefixed with a one-byte length indicator during DNS packet serialization. Properties Altitude Gets or sets the altitude component of the geographical position. The value is stored as a StringEncodedDouble, representing the altitude in meters. [DNSField(FieldsSizeOptions.PrefixedSize1B)] public StringEncodedDouble Altitude { get; set; } Property Value StringEncodedDouble Remarks The field is serialized with a one-byte length prefix. Latitude Gets or sets the latitude component of the geographical position. The value is stored as a StringEncodedDouble, representing the latitude in degrees. [DNSField(FieldsSizeOptions.PrefixedSize1B)] public StringEncodedDouble Latitude { get; set; } Property Value StringEncodedDouble Remarks The field is serialized with a one-byte length prefix. Longitude Gets or sets the longitude component of the geographical position. The value is stored as a StringEncodedDouble, representing the longitude in degrees. [DNSField(FieldsSizeOptions.PrefixedSize1B)] public StringEncodedDouble Longitude { get; set; } Property Value StringEncodedDouble Remarks The field is serialized with a one-byte length prefix.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks The binary format of the LOC record is defined as follows (each field is one or more octets): MSB LSB +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0 | VERSION | SIZE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 2 | HORIZ PRE | VERT PRE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 4 | LATITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 6 | LATITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 8 | LONGITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 10 | LONGITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 12 | ALTITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 14 | ALTITUDE | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ (octet) The fields are defined as: VERSION: A one-octet version number (must be zero). SIZE: The diameter of a sphere enclosing the described entity, in centimeters. It is represented as a pair of four-bit unsigned integers: the first (most significant) is the base, and the second is the exponent (power of 10). For example, 0x15 represents 1e5 cm. HORIZ PRE: The horizontal precision of the location (i.e., the diameter of the horizontal "circle of error"), expressed in centimeters with the same exponential representation as SIZE. To obtain the value in meters, divide by 100. VERT PRE: The vertical precision, representing the total potential vertical error, expressed in centimeters using the same exponential representation. To convert to meters, divide by 100. LATITUDE: The latitude of the location, stored as a 32-bit integer in network byte order, representing thousandths of a second of arc. A value of 2^31 indicates the equator; values above that are north. LONGITUDE: The longitude of the location, stored as a 32-bit integer in network byte order, representing thousandths of a second of arc, rounded away from the prime meridian (2^31). Values above 2^31 are east. ALTITUDE: The altitude of the location, stored as a 32-bit integer in centimeters, with an offset such that a value of altitudeZeroCorrection represents 0 meters relative to the WGS 84 spheroid. To convert to meters, divide by 100. The class also provides properties that convert the exponential representation into standard double values (e.g., meters for precision, seconds of arc for latitude/longitude, etc.), and vice versa. Properties Altitude Gets or sets the altitude of the location, in meters. The altitude is stored as a 32-bit integer (network byte order) in centimeters, with a base offset so that a specific value corresponds to 0 meters relative to the WGS 84 spheroid. public double Altitude { get; set; } Property Value double HorizontalPrecision Gets or sets the horizontal precision (diameter of the circle of error) in meters. Internally stored as centimeters in an exponential representation. public double HorizontalPrecision { get; set; } Property Value double Latitude Gets or sets the latitude of the location, in seconds of arc. The latitude is stored as a 32-bit integer (network byte order) where a value of 2^31 represents the equator. Values above 2^31 are interpreted as north. public double Latitude { get; set; } Property Value double Longitude Gets or sets the longitude of the location, in seconds of arc. The longitude is stored as a 32-bit integer (network byte order) where a value of 2^31 represents the prime meridian. Values above 2^31 are interpreted as east. public double Longitude { get; set; } Property Value double Size Gets or sets the size (the diameter of the sphere enclosing the entity) in centimeters. The value is stored internally in an exponential representation. public double Size { get; set; } Property Value double Version Gets or sets the version number of the LOC record format. This must be 0. [DNSField] public byte Version { get; set; } Property Value byte VerticalPrecision Gets or sets the vertical precision (total potential vertical error) in meters. Internally stored as centimeters in an exponential representation. public double VerticalPrecision { get; set; } Property Value double Methods ToString() Returns a string representation of the LOC record, including the geographic coordinates, altitude, and the size and precision of the location. public override string ToString() Returns string A formatted string representing the LOC record.

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks An SRV record indicates: Priority: A 16-bit integer indicating the preference for this target server. Lower values are more preferred. Weight: A 16-bit load-balancing weight. If multiple servers have the same Priority, clients should distribute connections proportionally based on these weights. Port: The TCP or UDP port on which the service is provided. Server: The domain name of the target host providing the service. For example, a typical SRV record might look like: _sip._tcp.example.com. IN SRV 10 60 5060 sipserver.example.com. This indicates that for the service _sip over TCP, example.com uses sipserver.example.com on port 5060 with priority 10 and weight 60. SRV records facilitate service discovery by allowing multiple servers to be specified, each with its own priority and weight. Clients look up the SRV record to find the hostname and port for the given service, potentially balancing load or choosing higher-priority servers first. Properties Port Gets or sets the TCP or UDP port on which the service is provided. [DNSField] public ushort Port { get; set; } Property Value ushort Priority Gets or sets the priority for this target host. Lower values indicate higher priority. [DNSField] public ushort Priority { get; set; } Property Value ushort Server Gets or sets the domain name of the target host providing the service. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName Server { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Weight Gets or sets the weight for load balancing among multiple targets that share the same Priority. [DNSField] public ushort Weight { get; set; } Property Value ushort Methods ToString() Returns a string summarizing the SRV record, e.g. "Priority Weight Port ServerName". public override string ToString() Returns string

+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ / EXCHANGER / / / +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ The fields are defined as: PREFERENCE: A 16-bit non-negative integer indicating the relative priority of this record among others for the same owner. Lower values indicate a higher priority. EXCHANGER: A domain name that specifies a host willing to act as a mail exchange for the owner name. This field is not compressed. When processing a KX record, DNS resolvers must include an additional lookup for the A record corresponding to the EXCHANGER field. If IPv6 is supported, the resolver should also look up the AAAA record for the exchanger. Properties Exchanger Gets or sets the domain name of the host that is willing to act as a mail exchange. This field specifies the target host for additional A (and AAAA) record lookups. [DNSField] public DNSDomainName Exchanger { get; set; } Property Value DNSDomainName Preference Gets or sets the 16-bit preference value for this KX record. Lower values indicate a higher preference when multiple KX records are present. [DNSField] public ushort Preference { get; set; } Property Value ushort

Validations.Variable<T>(T, string) Remarks A KEY RR can indicate a zone key (for DNSSEC), an end-entity key, or a user/account key. The Flags field includes bits that govern usage and key ownership, and can also signify the presence of a no-key state or an extension field. If both ProhibitedForAuthentication and ProhibitedForConfidentiality are set, then this record effectively asserts that there is "no key." This can be used to claim a zone is unsigned (insecure), or to prohibit usage for certain cryptographic functions. The Protocol and Algorithm fields follow RFC 2535 §3 definitions, and the public key (PublicKey) is algorithm-specific data. For further details on usage, see: RFC 2535 for DNS security extensions RFC 2536 (DSA keys) RFC 2537 (RSA/MD5) RFC 2539 (Diffie-Hellman) The RDATA is composed of: Flags (16 bits) indicating key usage, key owner type, and extensions. Protocol (8 bits) describing the intended use (DNSSEC, TLS, email, IPSEC, etc.). Algorithm (8 bits) identifying the signing algorithm. Public Key (variable length) containing the algorithm-specific key material. Properties Algorithm Gets or sets the cryptographic algorithm. Common values include 3 (DSA), 1 (RSA/MD5), etc. for DNSSEC usage. [DNSField] public Algorithm Algorithm { get; set; } Property Value Algorithm Extended Gets or sets a value indicating whether the "extension" bit (bit 3) is set, indicating additional 16 bits of flags are present in Extension. public bool Extended { get; set; } Property Value bool Extension An optional 16-bit extension field present only if Extended is set in the Flags. [DNSField(Condition = "Extended")] public ushort Extension { get; set; } Property Value ushort Flags Gets the 16-bit flags field which indicates key usage, key owner type, extension flags, and signatory bits. This is manipulated through properties such as ProhibitedForAuthentication, ProhibitedForConfidentiality, Extended, KeyOwner, and SignatoryField. [DNSField] public ushort Flags { get; } Property Value ushort KeyOwner Gets or sets the key owner (user/account, zone key, host/entity, or reserved). This corresponds to bits 6-7 in the flags. public KeyOwner KeyOwner { get; set; } Property Value KeyOwner ProhibitedForAuthentication Gets or sets a value indicating whether the key is prohibited for authentication use (flag bits 0-1 set to 10 in the specification). public bool ProhibitedForAuthentication { get; set; } Property Value bool ProhibitedForConfidentiality Gets or sets a value indicating whether the key is prohibited for confidentiality use (flag bits 0-1 set to 01 in the specification). public bool ProhibitedForConfidentiality { get; set; } Property Value bool Protocol Gets or sets the 8-bit protocol value. Typically 3 (DNSSEC), but can be TLS (1), email (2), IPSEC (4), or others as assigned by IANA. [DNSField] public Protocol Protocol { get; set; } Property Value Protocol PublicKey Gets or sets the raw public key bytes. This is algorithm-specific data whose format depends on the Algorithm. [DNSField] public byte[] PublicKey { get; set; } Property Value byte[] Remarks If the No-Key bits are set in Flags, the PublicKey should be omitted or ignored. SignatoryField Gets or sets the 4-bit signatory field (bits 12-15), indicating whether the key can validly sign updates (RFC 2137). public byte SignatoryField { get; set; } Property Value byte
